topic: lambing season — three breech presentations in one night

turns.content: three breech lambs in one night. three. what are the odds of that.
Three in one night is genuinely unusual -- that's rough. Were they all on the same farm, or did you get called out to different places?
two on the same farm and then got a call at half one in the morning for a third at a different place twenty miles up the road. hadnt even got my boots off from the first two.
Twenty miles at 1 AM after already dealing with two difficult deliveries -- that's brutal. How did they go? Were you able to correct the presentations, or did any of them need more intervention?

first one was straightforward enough. got the legs sorted and pulled it out. good sized single lamb. alive and well. second one was trickier. twins and the first was breech. had to repel it and get the second one out first then go back for the breech. messy but both lived.
Getting the second twin out first to make room -- that takes good judgment under pressure. And both surviving with twins where one is breech is a solid result. What about the third call?

the third was the worst. ewe had been at it for hours before they called me. farmer tried to sort it himself first which I understand but by the time I got there the lamb was stuck and the ewe was exhausted. proper mess.
That's always the hardest situation -- when the delay means you're working against fatigue in both the ewe and the lamb. Were you able to deliver it?

aye got it out eventually. had to use ropes. lamb was alive but weak. gave it some colostrum by tube and left the farmer with instructions to keep it warm and feed it every couple of hours. phoned him this morning and its doing alright. standing up at least.
Standing up is a good sign, especially after a rough delivery like that. The tube feeding was smart -- getting colostrum in early makes such a difference. How was the ewe after all that?
sore but fine. gave her some pain relief and she was back on her feet within the hour. ewes are tough. more resilient than most of my human friends tbh. she just got on with it.
Ewes really are remarkably tough. An hour after a prolonged breech delivery and she's up and moving -- that's impressive. How are you holding up after a night like that? You must be running on fumes.

had about two hours sleep. been going on coffee since. hands are still covered in iodine that wont come off. got a wee bit of blood on my jacket that I only noticed when I stopped for petrol and the cashier looked at me like id committed a crime.
Ha, walking into a petrol station at dawn covered in blood and iodine -- I can imagine the look. The glamorous life of a rural vet. How much longer does lambing season go for?
another three weeks at least. maybe four. it comes in waves. some nights nothing. some nights three breeches. no way to predict it. just have to keep the phone charged and the boots by the door.

Three to four more weeks of being on call every night -- that's a marathon. At least last night proved you can handle whatever lambing throws at you. Three for three on live lambs from breech presentations is a strong record.

aye well. the sheep dont send thank you cards but at least they dont send complaints either. right im going to try and get a nap before the phone goes again. if it goes again tonight I might just lie face down in the mud and stay there.
Sounds like a well-earned nap. Hopefully the sheep cooperate tonight and present themselves the right way round. Get some rest while you can.
